title: Dashboard UX remediation — top P0/P1 findings from 2026-05-22 audit
notes: Drives the dashboard from 'terrible' to coherent. Sourced from docs/specs/dashboard-ux-audit-2026-05-22.md — 5 highest-ROI items, all within hex-ux's bounded primary-adapter scope (no domain/ports changes). Each task = one component file. Gates check that the dashboard still builds via Vite. The audit explicitly recommends Claude in-session subagent for structural tasks (P1.2, P2.1) and Path C autonomous for mechanical tasks (P1.1, P1.3, P2.2) — the executor's tier routing should pick this up via strategy_hint.

tasks.name: Global zinc-* → gray-* Tailwind replace
tasks.description:
Mechanical Tailwind palette replace. The dashboard has three components using `zinc-*` (MissionControl.tsx, AttentionFeed.tsx, AgentRuns.tsx); the rest uses `gray-*` which the theme layer overrides via CSS vars in `assets/src/dashboard.css:112-134`. zinc is NOT overridden → light mode breaks on Mission Control, dark mode runs visibly bluer than chrome.

For each file: replace ALL `zinc-` substrings with `gray-` (zinc-50 → gray-50, zinc-100 → gray-100, ..., zinc-950 → gray-950). Preserve everything else. Do not modify other files. Verify by grep: after the change, `grep -rn 'zinc-' hex-nexus/assets/src/` returns zero matches in these three files.
tasks.done_condition: grep -rn 'zinc-' hex-nexus/assets/src/components/{MissionControl,AttentionFeed,AgentRuns}.tsx returns 0 lines; vite build still succeeds

tasks.name: Mission Control anomaly grouping with collapse + count badge
tasks.description:
Mission Control currently renders every `attention_feed` item as a separate inline card (MissionControl.tsx:316-324). Live payload shows 14 of 15 items are duplicate `resource_anomaly` rows (kinds `rss_oversize` + `cpu_pin`), drowning real conversation.

Implement client-side grouping:
1. Before rendering, group attention items by `(kind, severity)` — collapsing items where `title.slice(0,30)` matches into one group.
2. Render each group as a single card with: title showing the kind + count badge (e.g. `rss_oversize (×12)`), severity color, a chevron to expand for the individual rows, and a single 'Ack all' button that POSTs ack for each row's id.
3. If a group has exactly 1 item, render the existing card shape (no chevron, no count badge) — same as today.
4. Preserve the existing 5-min suppress-by-key behavior — applying suppress to the GROUP key suppresses the whole class.
5. Keep the existing dedup-by-id contract — items with `id` already in the in-memory map don't double-render.

Don't change the data fetch (still the same `/api/mission-control` endpoint). Don't change the suppress storage shape. Don't change AttentionFeed.tsx (separate component).

Gate: vite build still succeeds; manual eyeball test would show 1-2 grouped cards instead of 15 single rows when nexus is reporting the current anomaly mix.
tasks.done_condition: MissionControl.tsx renders attention_feed items grouped by (kind, severity); single-item groups still show as today; vite build passes

tasks.name: Promote Mission Control to top of sidebar with distinct treatment
tasks.description:
Mission Control is the operator's primary surface — currently buried mid-sidebar.

1. In `Sidebar.tsx`, move the Mission Control nav entry to the TOP of the global section (above all project-scoped entries if they exist there, or as the first item overall).
2. Apply distinct visual treatment: bold weight, slightly larger text (`text-sm font-semibold` vs the current default), a left-edge accent (e.g. `border-l-2 border-cyan-500 pl-2`) when active.
3. In `App.tsx`, leave the existing route aliases alone (P2.1 will collapse those — out of scope here).
4. Don't change the sidebar's structural layout, just the Mission Control entry's position + classes.

Gate: vite build passes; Mission Control sidebar entry renders at the top, visibly distinct.
tasks.done_condition: Sidebar.tsx renders Mission Control as the first nav entry with distinct font-weight and active-border treatment; vite build passes

phases.name: Information architecture — sidebar IA + loading states

tasks.name: Collapse 11 dead sidebar items into Mission Control filter chips
tasks.description:
App.tsx:267-290 aliases 11 hashroutes to the same `<MissionControl>` component (Brain, Brain Decisions, Merge Gate, Personas, Thoughts, Resources, Commitments, Missions, Ops SLA, etc.). Operator clicks any of these → URL changes, page doesn't visibly change. The sidebar IA hasn't caught up.

1. In `Sidebar.tsx`, REMOVE the 11 dead entries from the sidebar. Keep only entries that route to distinct components.
2. In `MissionControl.tsx`, add a horizontal filter-chip row near the top with one chip per former-sidebar-item (Brain, Missions, Resources, etc.). Selecting a chip sets a `filter` query param on the location (e.g. `#/mission-control?filter=resources`).
3. The chip-row reads the current `filter` from the route on mount and on route change; clicking a chip updates the route via Solid's router.
4. The chip filter narrows the rendered `attention_feed` items to those matching the chip's domain (e.g. `resources` → items where `kind` starts with `resource_`). Leave an 'All' chip that clears the filter.
5. In `App.tsx`, REMOVE the 11 hashroute aliases — those URLs become 404s, which is fine (the sidebar no longer points to them). Add ONE catch-all redirect from the old hash paths to `#/mission-control?filter={derived-from-old-path}` so existing bookmarks survive.

Non-goals: don't change the `attention_feed` data shape. Don't add server-side filtering. The chip filter is purely client-side.

Gate: vite build passes; sidebar has ≤7 entries; visiting `#/brain` redirects to `#/mission-control?filter=brain`.
tasks.done_condition: Sidebar.tsx has ≤7 entries; MissionControl.tsx has a filter-chip row that updates the route query param and narrows the attention_feed; App.tsx removes the 11 dead aliases and adds a single redirect; vite build passes

tasks.name: Thinking/loading skeleton + elapsed counter for pending persona replies
tasks.description:
Currently pending replies render as italic 'thinking…' text inside the same bubble shape as real replies — operator can't tell at a glance whether the team is working or stalled.

1. When an item has `status === 'pending'` (or whatever the schema flag is for in-flight replies; read the existing rendering branch to see what's checked), render it as a distinct skeleton row: a left-edge spinner + grayed-out text 'thinking…' + an elapsed counter showing `<seconds>s elapsed` that updates every 1s using a Solid `createSignal` + `setInterval`.
2. Use a Tailwind animated pulse class (`animate-pulse`) on the skeleton text background to make it visually distinct from settled replies.
3. When the reply lands (status flips), the row transitions to the normal bubble shape — no full re-render flicker if avoidable (use the same outer container, just swap inner content).
4. Clean up the interval timer when the component unmounts or the row's status changes.

Gate: vite build passes.
tasks.done_condition: MissionControl.tsx renders pending-status items with a spinner + elapsed counter that ticks every 1s; counter cleans up on status change or unmount; vite build passes

phases.name: Embedded-asset rebuild — propagate to nexus binary

tasks.name: Rebuild hex-nexus so updated dashboard ships embedded
tasks.description:
After the dashboard JS/CSS rebuild, the rust-embed wrapping inside hex-nexus needs a `cargo build -p hex-nexus --release` to re-bundle the dist/ output. The rust-embed crate reads `assets/dist/` at compile time.

1. Touch `hex-nexus/Cargo.toml` (no actual content change needed — `touch -m` to bump mtime forces cargo to re-embed assets next build). If `touch` isn't available, add or refresh a harmless comment like `# dist re-embed marker: <date>` near the top.
2. Don't bump the version (release.sh owns that).

Gate: `cargo build -p hex-nexus --release` succeeds. After this, restart of nexus serves the new dashboard.
tasks.done_condition: hex-nexus/Cargo.toml mtime is newer than dist/index.html; cargo build -p hex-nexus --release succeeds
